title: PHP-FPM Child Process Spawn vs Exit Rate
dataSources: High churn suggests `pm.max_requests` recycling, crashes, or thundering herd after deploy.
app: Splunk Universal Forwarder; optional Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (`Splunk_TA_nix`); custom `props.conf`/`transforms.conf` for `phpfpm:*` sourcetypes
spl:
index=web sourcetype="phpfpm:status"
| eval tc=tonumber(total_processes)
| sort 0 host, pool, _time
| streamstats window=2 global=f current=f last(tc) as prev_tc by host, pool
| eval delta_tp=tc-prev_tc
| timechart span=5m sum(abs(delta_tp)) as process_churn by pool
description: Rapid changes in `total_processes` indicate recycling, crashes, or aggressive scaling. Comparing churn to `pm.max_requests` distinguishes healthy recycling from faults.
value: Shortens MTTR for extension crashes misread as traffic issues.
implementation: Poll status frequently enough to see step changes; compare with `pm.max_requests` setting from config lookup.
visualization: Time series (pool utilization, queue, latency percentiles), single value alerts, top charts for scripts and methods.
grandmaExplanation: We watch your PHP worker pools so busy sites do not run out of hands to answer requests.
